nativeRange.habitat: Shaded blackwater forest streams and slow-moving or standing waters with abundant leaf litter, overhanging vegetation, and gentle current; water is tea-coloured from tannins and humic acids with very low mineral content

decorPreferences: dark substrate (fine sand or small gravel) to enhance colour
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannin release and cover
dense planting with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Cryptocoryne, Rotala)
floating plants to diffuse lighting and mimic forest canopy shade

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, insects, worms, and crustaceans. In the aquarium, accepts quality micro-pellets and fine flake as staples; supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods such as brine shrimp nauplii, daphnia, cyclops, and finely chopped bloodworms. Feed 1–2 small portions daily; avoid overfeeding in soft, blackwater setups where water quality can decline quickly.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetra, neon tetra)
dwarf gouramis and peaceful labyrinth fish
avoidWith: larger or boisterous fish that may outcompete or intimidate

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Rasbora einthovenii; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: The Long-Band Rasbora is a slender, mid-sized Southeast Asian schooler distinguished by a bold, unbroken black mid-lateral stripe running from snout to tail base. A blackwater specialist from Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo forest streams, it thrives in soft, acidic, tannin-rich water and rewards blackwater biotope setups with intensified colouration.
careNotes: Rasbora einthovenii is a blackwater specialist native to the shaded forest streams of the Malay Peninsula (including Singapore), Sumatra, Borneo, and southern Thailand. The species is best kept in soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 1–10) with a temperature of 22–26°C. Its most distinctive feature is a continuous, bold black mid-lateral band stretching from the snout to the caudal peduncle — more defined and unbroken than in many similar rasboras, and intensified when the fish is kept in tannin-stained water. Recreating blackwater conditions using driftwood, dried Indian almond leaves, or peat filtration is strongly recommended: colour, confidence, and long-term health are all notably improved. A dark substrate and floating or dense surface planting to subdue lighting mirrors the species’ shaded forest-stream habitat. Keep in schools of at least 8 — larger groups of 12+ produce more natural shoaling behaviour and reduce individual shyness. The species is an active mid-water swimmer and an accomplished jumper; a tight-fitting lid is essential. Gentle filtration (sponge filter or low-flow hang-on) is preferred — strong current is uncharacteristic of the natural habitat. Intermediate care level is warranted by the specific soft-water chemistry requirements; in hard or alkaline tap water, conditioning with RO/DI water or blackwater additives is necessary. The species is generally hardy once settled in appropriate water chemistry.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner with no parental care. Breeding in captivity is considered difficult. A separate, dimly lit breeding tank with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort) or spawning mops is recommended. Condition prospective pairs with live and frozen foods prior to spawning. Water should be very soft and slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 1–4, temperature 24–26°C). Adults will eat eggs; remove parents immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming within a further 24–48 hours. Feed fry infusoria or commercial fry food initially, progressing to micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii. Females are typically deeper-bodied than males.

primary.name: FishBase — Rasbora einthovenii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided maximum size (10.6 cm SL), temperature range (22–26°C), pH (6.0–6.5), hardness (dH 0–5), native range (Malay Peninsula and Indonesia), habitat (forest streams), diet (worms, crustaceans, insects), taxonomy (Danionidae/Rasborinae, Cypriniformes). Family retained as Cyprinidae per project convention. Adult aquarium size set at 6–8 cm based on corroborating hobbyist sources.

additional.name: Marine Life ID — Brilliant Rasbora (Rasbora einthovenii)
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Southeast Asia, Malaysia, Indonesia), slow-moving to stagnant vegetated waters, peaceful schooling temperament (groups of 6–10+), planted tank requirement. Used as corroborating source for habitat and social behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; requires separate breeding tank with soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5), fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, and removal of adults post-spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours. Fry require infusoria or commercial fry food initially. Conditioning with live foods improves spawn success.
